title: OIDC login redirect loop after Okta-to-Entra ID migration for users with plus-addressed or aliased emails
problem: After migrating SSO from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID, approximately 30% of users experience an infinite redirect loop when logging into TaskFlow. Users authenticate successfully at Microsoft but are immediately redirected back to the login flow upon returning to TaskFlow. The affected users correlate with those using plus-addressed emails (e.g., jane+taskflow@company.com) or having email alias differences between the two identity providers.
root_cause_hypothesis: TaskFlow's OIDC callback likely matches the authenticated user by comparing the email claim from the identity provider against stored user records. Entra ID is probably returning a different email format or a canonical email (stripping the plus-address suffix, or returning a primary alias) compared to what Okta returned. When the email claim doesn't match any existing user record, TaskFlow fails to establish a session and restarts the login flow, causing the loop. The 70% of unaffected users have simple email addresses that are identical in both providers.
reproduction_steps: Set up TaskFlow v2.3.1 (self-hosted) with OIDC pointing to Microsoft Entra ID
Create a user in TaskFlow whose stored email uses a plus-address (e.g., jane+taskflow@company.com) or an alias that differs from their Entra ID primary email
Attempt to log in as that user
Observe: authentication at Microsoft succeeds, but TaskFlow enters an infinite redirect loop instead of establishing a session
environment: TaskFlow v2.3.1, self-hosted, OIDC integration with Microsoft Entra ID, multiple browsers (Chrome, Edge, Firefox) — not browser-specific
impact: Approximately 30% of the team is completely unable to log into TaskFlow. This is a total blocker for affected users with no known workaround.
recommended_fix: Investigate how TaskFlow resolves the OIDC email claim to a local user account during the callback. Compare the email claim value returned by Entra ID against what Okta returned for affected users. Likely fixes: (1) normalize email comparison by stripping plus-address suffixes before lookup, (2) add support for matching on a secondary claim like `sub` or `oid` instead of relying solely on email, or (3) update affected user records to match the email format Entra ID returns. Also check that the session is actually being created on successful match — if the user lookup fails silently and no session is set, the auth middleware would restart the flow.
proposed_test_case: Write an integration test that configures OIDC with a mock provider returning an email claim of 'jane@company.com' for a user stored in TaskFlow as 'jane+taskflow@company.com'. Verify that the user is matched, a session is established, and no redirect loop occurs. Add a second case for alias mismatches (e.g., provider returns 'j.doe@company.com' but user is stored as 'jane.doe@company.com').
information_gaps: No server-side logs or error messages from TaskFlow during the failed login attempts
Exact email claim value Entra ID returns for affected users vs. what is stored in TaskFlow's user table
Whether TaskFlow logs or surfaces a user-not-found or session-creation-failed error anywhere
